metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

basic-turn asserts the smallest round-trip: a single plain-text user message, one assistant reply, no tool use, producing the `ready → working → ready` arc with `TotalTokens > 0`.

OpenCode supports a true headless turn: `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` accepts the prompt directly and blocks until the assistant turn completes. Session state lives in OpenCode's SQLite store (`~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db`), which the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls for the UUID-keyed `ses_…` session.

The daemon observes the full turn arc. The opencode parser maps OpenCode's `step-finish` part with `reason="stop"` to a `turn_done` event that closes the turn (parser.go: `case "stop": ev.EventType = "turn_done"`), driving the session back to `ready`. The committed sibling recording (`long-idle-live-session/events.jsonl`) confirms a single `opencode run` produces exactly the spec arc on one `ses_…` session: `new_state=ready` (new session created) → `working` (force ready→working on first activity) → `ready` (agent finished turn → ready). Token/cost data from step-finish populates a PerTurnContribution, so TotalTokens is non-zero.

The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) launches a blocking `opencode run` per `send` and chains `--session` for continuity; `wait_turn` is a no-op because the run already blocks. basic-turn is a single plain-text turn (one `send` + `wait_turn` + trailing `sleep`) — well within this grammar, so `driver_capability: ready`.

MODEL note: the column reassessment confirms opencode's default model is a local LM Studio model (`lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored`); the driver runs `opencode run` with no `--model` flag and uses that default. The model is small and slow (~30–45s/turn), so the recipe budgets generous timeouts.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.
The local gemma model is slow (~30–45s per turn); the recipe uses a 180s timeout so a slow turn does not trip a false timeout.

sources.note: parseStepFinish: reason="stop" → EventType "turn_done" closes the turn (working→ready); step-finish token/cost data builds a PerTurnContribution for all reasons except mid-turn tool-calls, so TotalTokens > 0.
Source variant ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) with ExactName{Name: "opencode"} process matcher; daemon polls the session table for the ses_… session.
Sibling recording: a single `opencode run` emits state_transition new_state ready→working→ready on one ses_… session — the exact basic-turn arc.
`opencode run` accepts a prompt directly and runs non-interactively (headless), blocking until the turn completes.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

The send is a no-tool single turn — opencode is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (no permission gate fires)

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow turn must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so working→ready is observed

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done closes the turn)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point (no tool call, no permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready
